summary: This notebook demonstrates a hybrid deep learning pipeline for scientific image forgery detection, combining a frozen DINOv2 vision transformer encoder with a lightweight custom CNN decoder for pixel-wise segmentation. It introduces Error Level Analysis (ELA) to amplify JPEG compression artifacts, fusing ELA predictions and heatmaps with standard RGB model outputs via weighted averaging. The pipeline applies gradient-based adaptive thresholding and morphological operations to generate binary masks, which are then RLE-encoded for submission. Readers should learn how forensic image analysis techniques can be effectively integrated with foundation models to improve forgery localization, particularly when dealing with subtle compression inconsistencies.
competition_flow: Loads test images, applies Error Level Analysis and a frozen DINOv2-based segmentation model to generate probability maps, fuses them with ELA signals, applies adaptive thresholding and morphological post-processing, and exports RLE-encoded masks to a CSV submission file.
data_reading: Reads images from the test directory using PIL.Image.open, converts them to RGB, and loads the sample submission CSV for merging.
data_processing: Resizes images to 518x518 and normalizes pixel values to [0,1].
Computes ELA by re-compressing at JPEG quality 90, calculating absolute differences, scaling, and converting to heatmaps.
Optionally applies test-time augmentation (TTA) via horizontal and vertical flips.
Post-processing includes gradient magnitude computation, Gaussian blurring, and morphological close/open operations for mask refinement.
features_engineering: Domain-derived ELA heatmap and compression artifact score, plus gradient magnitude features from the probability map used for adaptive thresholding.
ensembling: Combines RGB model predictions, ELA model predictions, and direct ELA heatmaps using a weighted sum, with optional test-time augmentation by averaging predictions from original and flipped inputs.
insights: ELA effectively highlights compression inconsistencies that correlate with image forgery.
Fusing forensic signals with deep features improves localization over using either alone.
Gradient-based adaptive thresholding outperforms fixed thresholds for mask generation.
critical_findings: ELA is only reliable for images originally saved as JPEGs, as applying it to lossless images introduces artifacts.
The author empirically determined a hidden JPEG detection threshold of 1.2 and an ELA compression quality of 90 for optimal artifact amplification.
